coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and characterization of bio-nanocomposite from sweet potato starch and Moringa oleifera leaf extract loaded with carbon quantum dots from Coffea arabica grounds
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

coredata.dc:creator: Nazar, Muhammad
Hasan, Muhammad
Wirjosentono, Basuki
Gani, Basri A.
coredata.dc:description: The study aims to generate and characterize a bio-nanocomposite film derived from sweet potato starch and Moringa oleifera leaf extract and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from Arabica coffee grounds employing the solvent casting method. The effect of various concentrations of CQDs (0–400 ppm) was investigated on the physicochemical properties, UV-barrier, antioxidant properties, and biodegradability of the bionanocomposite (BNC) films. The morphological surface of the bio-nanocomposite was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) were performed to evaluate the thermal stability of the samples. Mechanical properties, including tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB), were also assessed. The results show that the incorporation of CQDs with starch-based biofilms had a remarkable impact on the properties of BNC. SEM images confirm the homogeneous structure of the films with slight granulations. Colorimetric analysis indicates the increase in color difference of BNCs as the concentration of CQDs varied. The BNC100 formulation confirmed batter opacity, transparency, and UV-barrier properties while BNC50 possessed significant improvement in tensile, elongation as well as a substantial increase in the melting point. FTIR and XRD analysis confirmed proper chemical interactions among the components within the polymeric structure of the BNC film. In addition, CQD integration has also steadily enhanced the antioxidant properties and biodegradability of BNC films. The fabricated films with CQDs have demonstrated a better quality in terms of optical properties, physical properties, thermomechanical properties, biodegradation rate, and antioxidant behavior that suggest the film's application in food packaging or other relevant fields.

SEM images of bio-composite loaded with different concentrations of CQDs at 1000 magnification overlapped with the image of each sample observed under UV light (a) BC, (b) BNC50, (c) BNC100, (d) BNC200, (e) BNC400. UV Vis spectra of BNC expressed as (f) absorbance, (g) transmittance, and images of samples (h) water uptake capacity of BNC.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and characterization of bio-nanocomposite from sweet potato starch and Moringa oleifera leaf extract loaded with carbon quantum dots from Coffea arabica grounds

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study aims to generate and characterize a bio-nanocomposite film derived from sweet potato starch and Moringa oleifera leaf extract and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from Arabica coffee grounds employing the solvent casting method. The effect of various concentrations of CQDs (0–400 ppm) was investigated on the physicochemical properties, UV-barrier, antioxidant properties, and biodegradability of the bionanocomposite (BNC) films. The morphological surface of the bio-nanocomposite was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) were performed to evaluate the thermal stability of the samples. Mechanical properties, including tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB), were also assessed. The results show that the incorporation of CQDs with starch-based biofilms had a remarkable impact on the properties of BNC. SEM images confirm the homogeneous structure of the films with slight granulations. Colorimetric analysis indicates the increase in color difference of BNCs as the concentration of CQDs varied. The BNC100 formulation confirmed batter opacity, transparency, and UV-barrier properties while BNC50 possessed significant improvement in tensile, elongation as well as a substantial increase in the melting point. FTIR and XRD analysis confirmed proper chemical interactions among the components within the polymeric structure of the BNC film. In addition, CQD integration has also steadily enhanced the antioxidant properties and biodegradability of BNC films. The fabricated films with CQDs have demonstrated a better quality in terms of optical properties, physical properties, thermomechanical properties, biodegradation rate, and antioxidant behavior that suggest the film's application in food packaging or other relevant fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of conventional plastics has emerged as a global issue over the past few decades [1], predominantly attributable to their inherent resistance to rapid decomposition within natural ecosystems. Consequently, they present a significant risk of environmental pollution, encompassing soil and water contamination and disruption to aquatic biota [2]. Therefore, reducing conventional plastics use has become a prominent issue in developed countries worldwide. Moreover, scientists are intensively conducting studies and research on alternative biomaterial-based plastics that are more environmentally friendly [3].

Bioplastics or biofilms derived from biomass have been proven to be biodegradable [4], renewable [5], edible [6], and environmentally friendly [7]. Furthermore, some studies have reported upgrading bioplastics to active bioplastics [8] by adding bioactive compounds and nanoparticle fillers, resulting in active bio-nanocomposites. These advancements hold potential benefits in preserving food freshness, thus contributing to food safety [9].

Bio-nanocomposites are combinations of natural polymer matrices such as cellulose, chitosan, chitin, starch or amylose, polylactic acid, and polyhydroxyalkanoates with nano-fillers such as silica, alumina, Au, ZnO, TiO2, Cr2O3, and Fe2O3 [10]. Incorporating nanomaterials in biopolymer composites can ultimately enhance their thermal stability and mechanical properties due to nanomaterial's ability to transfer stress effectively, leading to improved tensile strength and stiffness [11,12]. However, most nanoparticle fillers used in bio-composite manufacturing are metals or metal oxides, posing a risk of adverse health effects because nanoscale materials can penetrate tissues and cells more rapidly, potentially leading to bioaccumulation in the human body over long-term usage [13]. For example, the 20 nm silver nanoparticles are known to be more toxic to the lungs due to faster penetration than the 100 nm-sized particles [14]. Therefore, developing environmentally and medically safe nanofillers is crucial for bio-composite fabrication [15].

CQDs are nanometer-scale carbon structures with unique optical and electronic properties, garnering significant attention for their potential applications in diverse fields [16–19]. The synthesis of CQDs using natural carbon sources offers a sustainable and eco-friendly approach, which is desirable for addressing environmental concerns associated with conventional nanomaterial [20,21]. Due to their being environmentally friendly and less toxic, CQDs can emerge as promising candidates for nanofiller materials in bio-composite fabrication [22]. Furthermore, incorporating CQDs in biofilms matrix offers enhancements in their mechanical properties [11,23], thermal stability [12], and optical properties [24]. Some studies also reported better UV-barrier capacity [25]and significance enhancement in antibacterial activity could be achieved when CQDs are introduced to the bio-composite [26–29].

In this work, the CQDs were incorporated as nano-fillers in polymer nanocomposites containing sweet potato starch, chitosan, and M. oleifera extract to investigate their effects on material properties, including thermal stability, mechanical strength, optical properties, water uptake, color change, and surface morphology of the BNC. The thermal stability of the BNC was evaluated using TGA and DSC. The film thickness, opacity, transparency, and UV barrier were evaluated using a micrometer and UV–Vis spectrophotometer respectively. In addition, the mechanical properties, including TS and EB, were evaluated using the proper apparatus.

Ethyl alcohol (96 %), glycerin, DPPH, and acetic acid were purchased from Merck, Germany, and used as received. Chitosan (DD = 80 %) Chimultiguna Co., Ltd. (Indramayu, Indonesia) with a particle size of 200–300 mesh, a molecular mass of 102 kDa, and used as received without further purification. CQDs were synthesized from the arabica coffee grounds through microwave irradiation, as previously reported and characterized [19,20]. Moringa leaf was obtained from the local market, the village of Cot Keueng, District of Aceh Besar, Aceh, Indonesia, and the sweet potato was purchased from the local market in Banda Aceh.

The bio-nanocomposite films were prepared using a solvent-casting technique, as reported by Hasan et al. [30]. Five different films containing chitosan, glycerin, sweet potato starch, and M. oleifera extract fortified by various concentrations of CQDs (0–400 ppm) were prepared at ambient temperature, and the resulting films were designated as BC, BNC50, BNC100, BNC200, and BNC400. Briefly, the CQDs and M. oleifera leaf extract at a desired concentration were mixed in a conical flask and stirred at 1500 rpm until a homogeneous mixture was obtained, sweet potato starch was then added, and the solution was heated up to 70 °C while stirring until a thick gelatinous mixture obtained. Subsequently, a definite amount of chitosan solution (dissolved in 0.1 M acetic acid, pH = 2.88) and glycerin were added to the matrix and stirred for 30 min. The matrix was then poured onto the glass surface (15 × 15 cm), stored in a desiccator for 12 h, and dried at ambient temperature for 24 h. After 24 h, the dried films were stored in airtight plastic bags for further analysis. The full scematic process of BNC fabrication is depicted in Fig. 1 .

Film thickness was measured at five different random spots on the BNC using a micrometer, and the means of samples were compared using ANOVA followed by Tukey's HSD test at a 5 % significance level. The film thickness was calculated using Equation (1):

Absorbance spectra of BNC films were measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer model, UV-1800 (Shimadzu, Japan). A 3 × 0.8 cm2 piece of BNC film was placed in a cuvette at ambient temperature. The absorbance was recorded at different wavelengths ranging from 200 to 800 nm and triplicated.

The UV-barrier capacity of the films was determined according to the method developed by Ref. [31]. Each sample film was prepared with dimensions of 3 × 0.8 cm, and the transmittance of the film was measured at the wavelength ranging from 200 to 800 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–Vis 1800, Shimadzu, Japan). The film opacity was determined from the absorbance of the film at 600 nm using equation (2):
(2)
O p a c i t y =
A x
Where, A is absorbance at 600 nm, and x is film thickness (mm).

Transparency can be measured using spectrophotometry with a wavelength between 200 and 760 nm. The transmittance value at a wavelength (λ 600 nm) was used to determine the transparency level of film samples. All samples were cut into 4 × 0.8 cm sizes and placed into cuvettes, and the transmission (T600) was recorded using a spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan). The transparency value is calculated using Equation (3).

Where T is the transmittance of a sample at 600 nm, and x is film thickness (mm)

The color of the BNC film was measured using a colorimeter app (colorimeter lab tool) running on an Android device (Xiaomi 12). Measurements were conducted in the CIELAB scale, each indicated as (L, a, b, and ΔE). The CIEL value indicates the lightness, while CIEa* and CIEb values refer to psychometric chromaticity, and the ΔE value denotes color change. All measurements were conducted under ambient temperature and replicated 5 times for each sample. ΔE was calculated according to the following formula:

Where L is the luminance, a is the chromaticity, and b is the hue. Subscripts s and b represent samples and blanks, respectively. ΔE is measured on a scale of 0–100, where 0 represents a small color difference, and 100 denotes complete distortion [32].

The microstructure of the bio-nanocomposite was visualized using a scanning electronic microscope (FlexSEM 1000, Hitachi, Japan). Each sample was cut into 0.5 cm2 size, immersed in liquid nitrogen, curio fractured, randomly broken, and then coated with gold palladium. All samples were observed under the following conditions: 5.6 mm working distance, 8 kV accelerating voltage, and magnifications ranging from 1000 to 10,000 times.

Mettler Toledo TGA (Ohio, United States) was used for thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA), and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was performed using a Mettler Toledo DSC821e. All samples (N = 5) were weighed between 3 and 6 mg. TGA and DSC analyses were conducted at 0 and 600 °C under nitrogen gas flow with a heating rate 10oCmin−1.

Tensile tests and elongation at break were performed using the CMT-10 Computer Control Electronic Universal Testing Machine, which complies with the American Standard (ASTM D638-14) with sample preparation type V [33].

Water uptake was performed to determine the film's resistance to water. Each film was cut to 1 cm2 in size and weighed. The dried film was then immersed in water for 2 h, and the surface of the film was gently rubbed with a tissue and weighed. The water uptake capacity was calculated using Equation (5).

The antioxidant assay was evaluated using 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH) following the procedure proposed by Kedare & Sigh, 2011 [34]. Briefly, DPPH powder was weighed and diluted in ethanol to obtain 0.1 mM of DPPH solution. In a series of test tubes or wells of a microplate, 2.0 mL of the 0.1 mM DPPH solution was added to each 1 ml of BNC solution. Each mixture was vortexed thoroughly to ensure proper mixing of the DPPH and BNC solutions, and the solutions were incubated in the dark at ambient temperature for 30 min to prevent DPPH photodegradation. After the incubation period, the absorbance of each sample was measured at 517 nm using a spectrophotometer. The percentage of DPPH radical scavenging activity is calculated using the equation:

Trend lines, line plotting, and calculation of average and standard deviation values were performed using OriginPro 2019b software (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, United States). Other statistical methods, including Tukey's HSD test and ANOVA, were performed using IBM SPSS Statistics 25 software (IBM Corporation, Armonk, New York, United States).

The optical properties of bioplastics are among the most crucial properties determining consumer acceptability [31]. The optical properties of biomaterials can be quite diverse and are influenced by various factors, including the composition, structure, and morphology of the bio-nanocomposite [35]. Depending on the composition and arrangement of the nanofillers within the polymer matrix, bio-nanocomposites can exhibit varying degrees of transparency, opacity, and UV-blocking ability. The BNC with varying concentrations of CQDs as nanofillers exhibits better optical properties than the control film.

Fig. 2 presents the optical properties of BNC enriched with CQDs at varying concentrations including film thickness, transparency, opacity, and UV barrier. Measurements of film thickness in Fig. 2a show that BNC100 is the thickest film (0.116 ± 0.005 mm) compared to other formulations, without statistically significant differences from BNC50 and BNC400, but significantly different from BC. The addition of CQDs has a notable impact on the resulting film thickness. The thickness of a film can be influenced by various factors including the deposition method employed (such as spin-coating or vacuum deposition), the concentration of the deposition solution, the deposition rate, substrate properties (like surface roughness), deposition temperature and pressure, solvent evaporation rate, interactions at interfaces between the film and substrate, and the presence of additives or surfactants in the deposition solution [36–38]. Generally, the BC and BNC films produced in this study meet the thickness requirements outlined in the Japan Industrial Standard, which sets the thickness of bioplastics at ≤0.25 mm [39].

Furthermore, the transparency and opacity of the films were also evaluated, as shown in Fig. 2b and c, respectively. The analysis results indicate that the film with the addition of 100 ppm CQDs possessed a better transparency value (0.15 ± 0.08 %), and the statistical test results show a significant difference with sample BC with a transparency of 0.11 ± 0.06 %. This transparency value is also confirmed by the opacity value, which is the opposite of transparency, where the film sample with the addition of 100 ppm CQDs has a lower opacity value than other formulations and statistically differs significantly from sample BNC400. An exciting finding from the transparency and opacity test results is that despite the high thickness of the BNC100 film, it maintained its low opacity and high transparency. The transparency value is one of the important properties of bioplastics when applied as food packaging because it determines consumer acceptance [40].

In addition, the ability to block UV is crucial for bioplastics to maintain food integrity [41], prevent photokinetic reactions [42], and avoid the formation of ROS due to excessive UV exposure [43]. The UV barrier capacity measurement Fig. 2d shows that BNC100 can reject UV (λ 280 nm) very well at 0.15 ± 0.05 and statistically differs significantly from the blank. The ability of BNC to reject UV rays is also confirmed by the low transmittance values of all formulations in the range of 200–350 nm as indicated in Fig. 3 g. The capacity of biofilms to block the UV may be associated with the chemical contents of M. oleifera extract, such as flavonoid, polyphenol, and other antioxidant species, and further enhanced by the CQDs' capability of absorbing UV and emitting visible light.

Table 1 shows the results of the color analysis of bioplastics based on the CIELAB scale. The mean was statistically compared using HSD Tukey's test (P < 0.05). Different superscripts denote significant differences among the samples. The result reveals fascinating insights into the effects of incorporating CQDs into bio-composite. Initially, comparing L* values across different samples indicates a significant alteration in the lightness of the color when CQDs are introduced. This result suggests that the concentration of CQDs plays a crucial role in determining the degree of lightness variation. The a* and b* values represent shifts along the red-green and yellow-blue axes, respectively, and similar trends emerge. While the control BC samples show relatively consistent values, introducing CQDs induces statistically significant alterations in both parameters. It is clear that adding the CQDs to the BNC alters the color change significantly, the greater the concentration of the CQDs the higher the difference in BNC sample color compared to BC.

The addition of CQDs into BC results in color changes in the films because CQDs are light brown, thus contributing to the color change. The addition of 50 ppm CQDs shows a significant color difference compared to the control. However, adding 100 ppm and 200 ppm of CQDs decreases the color change due to interactions between CQDs and the components of the bio-nanocomposite, including sweet potato starch, chitosan, and plasticizer. However, after reaching saturation, with the addition of 400 ppm CQDs, the color of the bioplastic differs significantly from the control with an E value of 21.99 %. These findings suggest a correlation between CQD concentration and color characteristics, with different concentrations yielding distinct shifts in color perception. Furthermore, the significant differences observed in ΔE values between the control BC sample and the BNC + CQDs samples reinforce the magnitude of color alteration induced by CQDs, with higher concentrations leading to more pronounced differences.

The surface morphology of the bioplastics was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to elucidate the distribution and arrangement of the CQDs within the polymer matrix. This analysis revealed a more uniform dispersion of the fillers, potentially impacting surface roughness, which could alter properties such as adhesion, wettability, and overall surface quality. Further characterization techniques such as atomic force microscopy (AFM) may provide additional insights into nanoscale surface features and their influence on the macroscopic properties of the bio-composites [44].

Fig. 3 shows SEM images of BC and BNC with the addition of CQDs at concentrations ranging from 50 to 400 ppm. The BC exhibits a smoother surface with few granules, as depicted by Fig. 3a. The SEM images also revealed that adding CQDs can significantly influence the composite surface by forming granules, likely originating from the incomplete dissolution of sweet potato flour. The addition of 50–400 ppm CQDs indicates an increasing granulation formation, as the red arrows pointed out. In addition to granules, bio-nanocomposites with the lowest addition of CQDs show the presence of pores (yellow arrows) in Fig. 3b, but the pores are not observed in other samples.

SEM analysis reveals a distinct morphological alteration in the surface structure of the composite due to the incorporation of CQDs. The smoother surface observed in composites without CQDs suggests a more homogeneous structure, while the appearance of granules in CQD-added composites indicates a phase separation, possibly due to the interaction between CQDs and the sweet potato flour matrix. The increasing abundance of granules with higher CQD concentrations suggests a dose-dependent effect. Furthermore, the presence of pores in composites with lower CQD concentrations implies a potential impact on the material's porosity, which may influence properties such as water permeability [2], water uptake, and mechanical strength [45]. Higher water absorption capacity, which is confirmed by Fig. 3h, where the BC loaded with 50 ppm of CQDs possessed the highest water uptake capacity.

The absorbance of BC samples with the addition of CQDs exhibits quite similar spectra, wherein significant absorption occurs in the UV region between 220 nm and 350 nm, as evidenced by the absorbance and transmittance spectra as depicted in Fig. 3f and g respectively, indicating that BNC is capable of effectively blocking UV light. The ability to reject UV rays is crucial for active bio-composites used for various purposes [41], particularly in food packaging [46,47]. UV rays have higher energy levels compared to visible rays, thus capable of altering food composition, triggering free radical reactions and photochemical reactions that can modify proteins, damage antioxidants, oxidize lipids, induce changes in color and composition, and produce undesirable tastes and odors [48,49]. Although proper UV usage can prevent bacteria in food, safeguarding against composition damage and the initiation of free radical reactions and photochemical reactions resulting in hazardous chemicals is imperative [50].

When two polymers are compatible or partially mixable, they can form specific chemical bonds between their chains throughout the polymer structure or at their interfaces [51]. These new bonds cause changes in the FTIR spectra of the composite material, such as shifts in bands and broadening of peaks, compared to the spectra of the individual polymers [40]. To explore potential chemical interactions between CQDs and BC polymers, the FTIR spectra of BC and BNC with varying concentrations of CQDs were analyzed (Fig. 4 a–c). This analysis focused on key regions associated with hydroxyl (νO−H) and carbonyl (νC=O) stretching vibrations, as well as (νN-H) and (νC-O) groups, due to their sensitivity to hydrogen bonding interactions.

In general, the incorporation of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) into the bio-composite matrix containing sweet potato starch, chitosan, and moringa leaf ethanol extracts demonstrates chemical interactions among several species within the polymeric structure of the BNC, as evidenced by the shifts in the wavenumber of OH absorption bands in the region of 3400 cm−1, peak sharpening in the N–H absorption region at 1536 cm−1, and the C–O absorption at 1414 cm−1 along with the formation of new carbonyl groups CO (1653 cm−1). The addition of CQDs induces a shift in OH absorption while broadening the FTIR spectra, as depicted in inset Fig. 5 b. The broadening and increased intensity of OH absorption attributed to hydrogen bond interactions [52] facilitated by the hydrophilic nature of CQDs, which readily dissolve in water. Thus, from Fig. 4b, it is noticeable that the higher the CQDs content in the bioplastic matrix, the stronger the signal of hydroxyl group absorption. Furthermore, adding CQDs also influences the sharpness of N–H and C–O absorption spectra and the appearance of CO group absorption at 1653 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 4c. The presence of CO groups, although weak, and the sharpening of N–H and C–O absorptions indicate the contribution of added CQDs, as CQDs typically possess CO and N–H groups on their surfaces as common characteristics [53,54], which induce photoluminescent properties by absorbing UV light and emitting visible light [55–58].

In addition to using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), the chemical interactions of the components in the bio-nanocomposite can also be examined through its crystallinity properties via X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis as depicted in Fig. 4d and e. The crystallinity of the (BC) and bio-nanocomposite (BNC) samples was evaluated using the LabX XRD-6100, Shimadzu, Japan. The diffractogram results indicated an enhancement in the crystallinity of the BNC with the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at varying concentrations. The diffractogram revealed that BC exhibited more amorphous characteristics, whereas the introduction of CQDs intensified several peaks in the XRD spectra, signifying alterations in BNC crystallinity. BNC100 exhibits greater crystallinity compared to the native and other formulation due to crosslinking interaction between components in the BNC with CQDs via formation of hydrogen bonding, the chemical properties of loaded CQDs as confirmed by the FTIR spectra supports this assumption.

The analysis of the diffractogram peaks demonstrated that the BNC synthesized in this study exhibited a body-centered cubic (BCC) structure, with three principal peaks observed at 8.32°, 11.26°, and 18.14° (2θ). Overall, it can be concluded that adding CQDs enhances BC's crystallinity, as evidenced by the emergence of new diffractogram peaks. Furthermore, increased CQD content correlates with a higher intensity of the XRD signals in the BNC, thus enhance the crystallinity.

Thermogravimetric analysis, also known as thermogravimetry (TG), is a method used to measure the mass of a polymer while it undergoes controlled temperature changes in a controlled environment [59–61]. Commercial TGAs typically cover a temperature range from room temperature to over 1000 °C, which is suitable for various polymer applications [62]. A purge gas, such as nitrogen, argon, or helium, is passed through the balance to create an atmosphere that can be inert, oxidizing (like air or oxygen), or reducing (gas formation). However, reducing atmospheres is seldom required for polymers. The TGA analysis results did not show significant differences in weight loss for BNC due to the addition of CQDs with varying concentrations, as seen in Fig. 5a. The polymer weight loss in the 25–110 °C temperature range indicates the evaporation of volatile materials and solvents, including glycerol, acetic acid, and water. Generally, all samples began to significantly lose weight upon heating in the temperature range of 220–345 °C.

The DSC analysis (Fig. 5b) reveals shifts in the glass transition temperature (Tg) and melting point ™ of the composite concerning the addition of CQDs at different concentrations. As depicted in Fig. 5d, adding 50 ppm CQDs to BNC significantly shifts the Tg from 49 °C to 59 °C. This shift in Tg is associated with an increased Tm value [63], as indicated by the red spectrum in Fig. 6 e. Generally, the DSC analysis results show an increase in the bio-nanocomposites' Tg and Tm values due to the addition of CQDs. However, at a concentration of 400 ppm, an anomaly occurs where both Tg and Tm values decrease, even falling below the Tg and Tm values of the blank sample, which may indicate saturation of the bio-composite with CQDs, and due to the hydrophilic nature of CQDs [64], BNC 400 becomes more brittle, prone to fracture, and exhibits lower EB values, as confirmed by the mechanical property testing data (Table 2 and Fig. 6)

The mechanical property testing indicates that adding CQDs to the BC can significantly enhance the bio-composites tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) as depicted in Table 2 and Fig. 6. The data reveals that the tensile strength of BNC50 is notably higher compared to the control and other samples with higher concentrations of CQDs. The TS and EB values enhanced up to 54.56 % and 131.7 % when reinforced with 50 ppm of CQDs. Statistical analysis using Tukey's HSD test shows that the TS values of BNC50, BNC100, and BNC200 are significantly different from the control (p < 0.05), whereas the TS value of BNC400 is not significantly different from the control. Furthermore, the EB values also exhibit significant improvement, especially for BNC50 and BNC100, with respective EB values of 33.44 % and 17.09 %, significantly different from the EB value of the control bio-composite (BC). These results elucidate that adding CQDs as nanofillers into the bio-composite matrix can significantly enhance the mechanical properties of the bio-composite, with an optimal dosage of 50 ppm. This finding is slightly better than previous work reported by Navidfar et al., 2022 [65].

The enhancement in mechanical properties due to CQD incorporation can be attributed to several key factors. Firstly, the uniform dispersion of CQDs within the bio-composite matrix likely creates a more homogenous material, reducing stress concentrations and improving tensile strength and elongation at break [66]. The increased surface area provided by CQDs facilitates stronger interfacial interactions between the nanofillers and the polymer matrix, enhancing load transfer efficiency under mechanical stress [12,45,67]. Additionally, the optimal concentration of 50 ppm appears to provide the finest balance between CQD reinforcement and preserving the bio-composite's inherent flexibility. Higher concentrations may lead to agglomeration of CQDs, which could act as stress concentrators and adversely affect the mechanical properties. These findings underscore the potential of CQDs as effective nanofillers and highlight the importance of optimizing filler concentration to achieve superior material performance. Future studies could explore the long-term stability and durability of these bio-nanocomposites under various environmental conditions and their potential applications in fields requiring high-performance biodegradable materials.

In recent years, bio-nanocomposites development has garnered significant attention due to their potential applications in various fields, including food packaging, biomedical devices, and environmental protection [42]. These materials combine the beneficial properties of biopolymers with the unique functionalities of nanomaterials, resulting in enhanced performance characteristics. One area of particular interest is the incorporation of natural antioxidants into bio-nanocomposites to improve their oxidative stability and extend their functional lifespan. Among the various natural antioxidants, Moringa leaf extract is renowned for its high content of bioactive compounds, such as flavonoids, tannins, and phenolic acids, which exhibit strong free radical scavenging activity [68,69]. The integration of CQDs into these bio-nanocomposites not only leverages the antioxidant properties of the Moringa extract but also enhances the overall material properties due to the unique surface chemistry and high surface area of CQDs.

Fig. 7 presents the results of the antioxidant activity assay, expressed as the percentage of DPPH inhibition, with vitamin C as the positive control. The antioxidant capacity of the bio-nanocomposite (BNC) is attributed to the inclusion of Moringa leaf extract, added in equal amounts by weight to each formulation due to its active constituents, such as flavonoids, tannins, and phenolic compounds, which act as effective agents against free radicals. The addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) positively enhanced the antioxidant activity of the BNC. According to Tukey's ANOVA and HSD tests at a significance level of 0.05, all BNC formulations showed a significant difference in antioxidant activity compared to both BC and vitamin C.

Adding CQDs increases the surface-to-volume ratio of the BNC, thus enhancing the polymer-filler matrix interaction. The interaction between CQDs and the active compounds in Moringa leaves is uniformly distributed throughout the biofilm matrix and on its surface, enhancing its interaction with DPPH. Consequently, the inhibitory activity increases with the concentration of CQDs. The rise in antioxidant activity is further attributed to the intrinsic ability of CQDs to inhibit oxidation. This finding is supported by the research of [64], who evaluated the antioxidant capacity of CQDs synthesized from fruit skin waste using the DPPH method. Their study demonstrated that CQDs exhibit significant antioxidant activity, even at concentrations of 3–5 mg/mL, surpassing the efficacy of vitamin C in scavenging hydroxyl radicals.

The biodegradability test of bioplastics formulated from sweet potato starch, chitosan, and CQDs derived from Arabica coffee waste is crucial in evaluating their environmental impact and sustainability. This test determines the rate and extent to which these bioplastics decompose under natural conditions (soil burial technique), providing insights into their potential to reduce plastic pollution compared to conventional plastics. Assessing biodegradability ensures that these materials will break down into non-toxic byproducts, supporting the development of eco-friendly alternatives that align with global efforts to minimize waste and promote a circular economy. Such evaluations are essential for validating the practical application and regulatory compliance of these innovative bioplastics. The biodegradability test was carried out at ambient temperature for 25 day of soil burial, the weight loss and serial photograph were documented every 5 days as can be observed in Fig. 8 .

Fig. 8 shows the weight loss of BC and BNC after being buried in the soil for 25 days. Every 5 days, each sample was weighed and photographed to obtain biodegradability progress data. The BNC weight measurement data showed that BNC50 degraded faster and more steadily, followed by BNC200 and other formulations. After 25 days of burial, BNC50 remained less than45 %, while other formulations remained >45 %. Fig. 8 also shows photos of each BC and BNC sample buried in the ground every 5 days. The images confirm that BNC50 showed a more pronounced weight loss on day 25. The results of this test correlate with the water uptake data in Fig. 3h, which shows that BNC50 has a relatively higher WU than other formulations. The high WU causes a film to absorb water faster and degrade more easily in the soil because the rate of biodegradation of a bioplastic is usually related to several factors, including the physicochemical properties of the bioplastic, thickness, water absorption, and soil moisture where the test is carried out. The biodegradability rate of BNC complies with the standard and is slightly better than previous work as reported by Panatarani et al., 2023 [70].

This study successfully incorporated CQDs synthesized from Arabica coffee waste into bio-composites consisting of purple sweet potato starch, chitosan, and Moringa leaf extract. The BNCs were prepared via the solvent evaporating method followed by physical characterization examining morphology, optical properties, and thermomechanical properties i.e. thermal decomposition, tensile, and elongation at break. Composite crystallinity and chemical properties were analyzed using XRD and FTIR respectively. Optical property analysis revealed that BNC100 exhibits better optical properties and water uptake compared to other formulations, whereas BNC50 shows remarkable thermal stability, mechanical properties, and biodegradation rate as evidenced by higher TS and EB values, enhancement in the melting point by up to 20.4 % compared to the native BC, and better weight loss performance. Antioxidant assay proves that CQDs contribute positively to the antioxidant behavior of the BNC. Overall results indicate that CQDs integration in BNC exhibits excellent UV-barrier and optical properties, and improves their thermomechanical properties, biodegradability, and antioxidant behavior.

Future research on bio-composites incorporating CQDs synthesized from Arabica coffee waste should focus on scaling up production, evaluating long-term stability, and exploring industrial applications, including sustainable packaging and biomedical uses. Further studies should investigate the mechanistic interactions at the nanoscale, enhance functional properties through surface modifications, and assess biodegradation pathways. Additionally, exploring the potential of BNCs in environmental remediation, flexible electronics, and energy storage could lead to significant advancements. Ensuring regulatory compliance and conducting toxicological assessments will be crucial for commercializing these innovative materials.

Muhammad Nazar: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Muhammad Hasan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization. Basuki Wirjosentono: Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis. Basri A.
